The term 'reverse purchasing' (or 'reverse purchase') is not used in mainstream US media. It appears predominantly in Chinese state media as a literal translation for a specific trend of foreigners buying Chinese goods, or in very niche real estate/mortgage contexts ('HECM for purchase'). No New York Times articles currently use these exact phrases, and major Western media typically refer to this trend as 'reverse consumption' or 'reverse haitao'. Given the short 22-day resolution window (June 9 to July 1, 2025) and the 6-week gap between the 'People's Daily' article (April 30, 2025) and the start of the window, it is highly unlikely that NYT will publish a story using this precise, non-standard translated phrase. I am estimating the probability at the minimum allowed bound (2%).

The base rate of authoritarian governments unblocking Western social media platforms after officially banning them is very low, unless the platform complies with local censorship demands (as seen with X in Brazil). Maduro temporarily banned X for 10 days in August 2024. A search for news of the ban being lifted or expiring found no results, whereas the lifting of Brazil's ban on X and Venezuela's ban on Telegram were widely reported. This strongly indicates X remains blocked in Venezuela as of mid-2025. China, Iran, Myanmar, North Korea, Russia, and Turkmenistan have deeply entrenched bans that are highly unlikely to be lifted by June 2025. Wikipedia is actively maintained and reliably groups these countries under the 'Current' bans section. The primary risks are either a quiet unblocking of X in Venezuela or Wikipedia restructuring the article in a way that disrupts the resolution criteria, but the Metaculus admins' fine print ensures they will seek a sensible resolution. Therefore, I estimate a 75% probability that all 7 countries will still be listed as currently blocking X.

Uruguayan President Yamandú Orsi received a direct public invitation from Brazilian President Lula da Silva in early March 2025 to attend the BRICS summit in Rio de Janeiro (July 2025). High-level Uruguayan government sources confirmed to local media (El País) that Orsi plans to attend as a guest, fulfilling Lula's desire for closer regional ties. Furthermore, on March 6, Uruguay's Foreign Minister Mario Lubetkin publicly characterized the BRICS invitation as an 'opportunity' to foster dialogue with key global players, strongly signaling the government's intention to accept. Given Orsi's left-wing alignment with Lula, the importance of Brazil as Uruguay's neighbor and trade partner, and the clear preparatory steps taken by the Foreign Ministry to justify attendance (positioning it as dialogue rather than bloc accession to placate US relations), it is highly likely that an official communication confirming his in-person attendance will be issued prior to the July 1, 2025 deadline.

Francis Suarez stated in early May 2025 that he was still considering a run for Florida governor in 2026. However, he heavily caveated this by pointing out that Donald Trump had already endorsed another candidate (Byron Donalds) for the position, which made the proposition "hard" and suggested he was looking at "other ways to serve." Trump's explicit "Complete and Total Endorsement" of Donalds in February 2025 has effectively framed Donalds as the dominant frontrunner. Exhaustive searches from May into early June 2025 show no formal campaign launch or any signs of an imminent announcement by Suarez. With the resolution deadline of July 1, 2025, just around the corner and Suarez openly acknowledging the grim political realities of running against Trump's primary pick, the probability of him jumping into the race in the next three weeks is very low.

Casey DeSantis was considered a strong potential candidate for the 2026 Florida gubernatorial race early in 2025. However, momentum shifted sharply in May 2025 following Donald Trump’s early endorsement of Byron Donalds for the GOP nomination, polling showing DeSantis trailing Donalds by double digits, and a criminal investigation into her 'Hope Florida' welfare program. Reports from mid-May (e.g., Miami Herald on May 14) indicate she is 'leaning against it' and political chatter has died down. On May 29, she also noted that voters are 'tired of politics' just 100 days into the new presidency. Furthermore, historically, Florida gubernatorial candidates (like Ron DeSantis in 2018 or Rick Scott in 2010) tend to announce in the election year itself, making an early-to-mid 2025 announcement unconventional. With only a few weeks remaining until the July 1, 2025 deadline and significant political headwinds currently deterring her, an announcement in this narrow window is extremely unlikely. I set the probability at 3%.
